speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, interview me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, here we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four with Sandor Klein. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was talking abut Mauthausen Concentration Camp. We visited this camp the later years, when our son was 17 years or 18-years-old-- 17. He wanted to see the past, and he wanted to know all this story. He was a history major, anyway, in his college years, and he was interesting what his family and what his people-- the Jewish people-- went through. We went to visit the camp. The camp is turned into a museum today, the same way they have it in Auschwitz. The crematorium-- I didn't mention before-- that was attached to Mauthausen, really the-- the place name was Gusen, G-U-S-E-N. That's where the crematorium was hiding. We were driving down from Vienna, and looking for the city of Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you believe it? Nobody-- but nobody-- said that, yeah, we know where is Mauthausen. There was no such a thing. I said, don't tell me. With my German, I tried to be calm and finding the-- the proper words. I saw that there was a woman out-- standing out in a-- in a gate, in a house. I said, where is the camp? Don't tell me there was no such a thing because I was here. And my wife who is standing next to me was in the same camp. And we know. There was no such a thing. Don't even talk about it. I don't know how, finally, we found it. It's in the same place where it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the tents-- the blue and white tent-- is still alive. The officers' barracks, which were their home, are still there. The gas chamber is there. The crematorium is there. The shower is there. We went into each and every room. I took-- not pictures. I took slides from there. I still have them in order somewhere down in the closet. Never looked since then. We even saw the window with the glass installed there where the soldiers was looking in if the people are already dead, so they can open the door and ship the people from the gas into the crematorium. We saw the shower rooms where, from the first, that instant of water, the gas came down. We saw the back room where they put the gas and mixed it, whatever mix they used to the Zyclon. And later on, as I was told-- I didn't see this myself-- they were burning people in there. Not the Jewish people, because Mauthausen was put up years, years before the-- the Jewish question came up. Greek, Polish, and, I think, gypsy people were over there. And they were burned alive. They were marched into the fire. They had a two-story crematorium building. People were feeding the fire downstairs, just like when we do barbecue today. The flames were coming through the steel bars. And people-- God bless you. And the people just marched in to their death. Some of them even fighting who will get in first. They want to get over it. And then, we walked up the steps, which the people had to build from the quarry, the big 70 kilogram which the sign said over there that each and every stone had to be carved out into a 70 kilogram piece. And they made the steps from it. We walked at the edge, where we were told that people marched and fell into the quarry to the death. We went the so-called museum, where they had pictures of the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] not the ruler-- the leader of the camp. His picture was on the wall. And the method of killing, what they did over there. We saw a hypo needle, which had about a eight, nine inches long needle. They put this through a live person's chest, and they injected some kind of a liquid. They wanted to find out, how long does it take to die from this, whatever they did. We saw the stretcher table over there, where people were tied by the leg and by the neck. And they were just stretching until their body popped into two sections. We saw over there the-- the whip that they used punishing people. We saw over there pictures for dead bodies, after they were stealing potato peels behind the kitchen. We saw people, like, glued to the electrical wire fence. They were running into the fence just to get over with the life. We saw pictures from the guard tower. You could see the front of the machine gun, the sparks just flying-- flying all over from the shooting. We saw people marching in the-- we saw pictures of people marching into the camp. We saw the camp, the latrine, the so-called washroom, the so-called room where they collected bodies-- the dead bodies every morning before they went for the so-called Appell, where they counted the persons who were still alive. According to the people-- I was not there, because I started to work 4:00 in the morning. But when the so-called morning breakfast Appell was done, they counted the heads, how many heads are still alive in the tent. That's how they brought out the so-called soup, which was made out of straw, carrots, and turnip. That was the daily-- daily food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the-- not manager of the tent reported every person, dead or alive, just to get a cup more of this so-called soup. And when the breakfast time was over, they carried out the body and went into the mass grave, one layer, one layer, one layer, one on the top of it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anything else I can talk about it? What happened in 1956?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1956, we had the revolution. I was not an active part of it, because after we went home from the concentration camp, I decided that I am not going to be part of any organization, anything except my-- my congregation. I don't want to be part of any part, group, nowhere. I just want to live my life the way I think is the right way to live for me. But my wife's parents lived about 100 kilometers from Budapest. And we didn't hear ever from them. The revolution went on from end of October till almost middle of November.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1956. And I was young. I was brave. I said, OK, I will go, somehow, and see what your parents are doing. So I said good-bye, and I went on. I went on in the street. And people were calling, let's go! Let's go to the radio station! There are trucks. I thought, there is a truck. Maybe they're going somewhere. I went on one of the truck, and we went to the radio station. Not that I wanted to go there, but we went there. And that's where the revolution started. The young people from the university asked the president of the radio station to let us read through the radio, so people will hear it everywhere, what are we-- I can say-- demanding from the university, our freedom. That was under Communist pressure. And there was a big commotion at the gate. They were-- the Hungarian-- how they call that-- the AVH group. That was the elite group of the Communist party. Somehow, they started some fight. And there came a woman with a baby in her arm. And somehow, a bullet flew-- nobody knew from where-- and the baby was killed. That started the revolution. People stormed the gate. People opened the gate and went in too. And I didn't hear it, but they claimed that they read all whatever they wanted to say. And the truck went by. I went on the truck. People were sitting inside the cab and on the back of the truck, and a bunch of weapons was on the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: I says, what are you doing this? We are bringing these weapons onto-- OK, can I go with you? Sure. So I was stuck on the truck. I went down, and we arrived to the city of Sarkad. The Russian soldiers stopped us. They put us in solitary jail, each and every one of us, without any question. That was around 7:00 at night. They took everything away from us-- shoe-laces, belt, identification book, watch, ring, anything which movable. We are put in solitary. And then, at around 4:00 in the morning, the guard walked by. And I asked the guy, are we going to get something to eat? I am very hungry. He looked around and said, like-- like he was talking very, very small.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was afraid, maybe, to say, be happy if they don't give you anything to eat. I didn't know what it meant. He walked by. We couldn't go out to the restroom. Around 8:00 in the morning, we were called one-by-one into the office, and we were told, pick out your items and get lost. So then, we find out, if we get-- if we would get something to eat, that mean-- that meant that we would have to stay in prison longer. So they let us out. And I was so brave from Sarkad, I went on to Mako to visit-- visit my wife's parents. They were good. And then, I cannot tell you don't now how. I don't remember. That's out of my mind. Either on bus or car or truck or whatever, but I went home. And I told my wife, that's it. That was enough what I saw, what I went through. That's enough. And that time, it was already in December when the revolution failed, and everything went back to so-called normal under Communist pressure. My brother came down to Budapest. He was the chief rabbi at the time in Jur, and he wanted to talk in the leadership in Budapest with the Jewish coalition-- congregation, wherever he went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he got stuck in our house. And then, we were talking and talking. And he said, you know what? We want to leave the country too. We want to go to Israel. My brother was the underground Zionist leader in the city of Ujpest in the younger-- younger years. He wanted to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I don't care where we're going. I have nobody nowhere. We are going. Let's go together. So he went home. The couple days later, we managed-- we said good-bye to our father. And then, the three of us-- our son was three and 1/2 years old that time-- we got a little-- one set of clothing for each of us. And we went up to my brother's place, which was fairly close to the Austrian border. And we got-- we found some connection to farmer, naturally. Good money. He said, I will take you across the border. We left his house around 11:00 at night. He said, there will be about an hour march until we reach the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to the bor-- we went to the border. And he told us, here is a river. It was not too wide. We didn't know how deep it is. Cross the river. I'm going back now. And then, follow this shiny star over there. That's already in the free world. That's in Austria. That will be a church. You just keep on going until you reach the church.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went into the water. We didn't know how deep it is. Thank God, it was not too deep. It was about a little over our knee. We crossed the river, and our son woke up. We gave him a sleeping pill on the Hungarian side. He slept through the whole thing. And we met other people who came from-- that was at the city of Andau, A-N-D-A-U. That's where we crossed the border. And about less than an hour later, a bus on the road picked us up. He said, don't worry. We are from the church. We will take you in the social hall. And then, next day, you can be on your own. And we were in Vienna for about two months. And we got the permit to come out to the United States under the Eisenhower presidency. We got the temporary permit to come in with then, I think, it was 32,000 people let in from Hungary. And we arrived to Brooklyn Harbor, 194-- I'm sorry, 1957, February 15th. That's when we arrived in the United States. And from there, we went to the army camp, was in New Brunswick, New Jersey. Camp Kilmer, that was the place. And over there, we got contacted by the Joined, by the HIAS, and by the Red Cross. And after a couple of weeks we spent over there, we went to Springfield, Massachusetts, and that's where we started our life. And thank God. [CRYING] I thank God every day that we are here. [SOBBING] And all this I was talking is just bad, bad, bad memories. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we are here. And we are retired. We settled in this security community. And hopefully, we will live out our days as far as God let us go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your son today?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in Kenosha, Wisconsin. And he is working over there at the Carthage College. He is a vice-president for admission. He finished his schooling. He received his master's degree here in nearby Long Beach University. He worked here a couple day-- a couple years. And then he moved to Vermont-- state of Vermont. He worked over there in the-- in a military college.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he married. And they have a girl who is our granddaughter. She is 15 years old now. She was 15-years-old in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Kelli, K-E-L-L-I. And she is going to school. And she lives-- she lives her life as an average American teenager. She is happy. She is learning languages. She is learning French, and also learning history. And she is very interested in the Holocaust, what was before and what happened after. We just talked to her last Sunday. And she thought we had this interview before. And she wanted to know what's going on. Now-- now she is in an age where she could digest all these things. Not the way I do, not the way you feel about it. But in her own way, I am sure she will have something to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandmother on my father's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Amelia Weiss. A-M-E-L-I-A. The last name is W-E-I-S-S. Amelia Weiss. She lived in the same town where I was growing up, in the city of Ujpest. And this is a picture which was taken a very old Volklander camera. That was a German camera. And that was taken in the year of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ujpest, the city of Ujpest. That's in Hungary. And you can see on this picture why I mentioned we will follow it later. The crippled arthritis on both of her hands-- she suffered for this many, many years, as I can remember. And we had a--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, we see a picture. It's a cover of a book. The book is talking about the years 1943, '44, and 1945 in Hungary, where the Jews were concentrated in ghettos. And in 1944, they were shipped out to Auschwitz. We found this book under-- next to the picture, you can see over there numbers and letters. That's 1-0-0 K-E-P. That's in Hungarian. It says 100 pictures. This book contains 100 pictures. And that was-- this book was put out in New York City here in the United States. And we got hold of it not too long ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's the person in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the person, the same we showed the other little picture. This is my grandmother of my father's side. And she is standing in front of a cattle wagon just minutes before they were shipped from Ujpest to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name one more time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Amelia Weiss. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the year 1944, July. That when this picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On these pictures, we see my father, whose name is Jeno Klein. And on the right side, we see my mother, whose name was [? Reich ?] Serena in Hungarian. The pictures were not taken exactly the same time. My father's picture was taken around 1964. He was visiting us here in the United States in 1963, and he went home. He let this picture taken and send it back to us with the words, so you will remember of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken back in Hungary, in Budapest. The-- my mother's picture is an old picture. You can see that's not exactly a picture. There's a hand-sketching out of a picture. There were seven of them, sisters, and they were together in time. That was in the early 1940s when this picture was taken. She was, that time, around 40. And she was transported out to Auschwitz, as I told before, in 1944, July 8, at the same time that my father's mother-- so my grandmother-- was taken out to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your mother's picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Ujpest, where we lived. That was a family get-together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. And go. Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sandor Klein. In Hungarian, it was Klein Sandor. And that was taken in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Ujpest, in my-- in my home town. That was my best time of the years. I can remember back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me on this picture. My name is Sandor Klein. And this picture was taken in Hungary, Budapest, in the year of 1949 or 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On this picture, you can see me-- my name is Sandor Klein-- and my wife that day. Her name is Susan Klein. We were married in 1952, July the 5th. If the Communists would know that this picture was taken in Hungary, right after a hidden religious wedding ceremony-- because that was one of the biggest sins you can commit in the Communist world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came out. That was taken in my brother's synagogue, the yard of the synagogue. That's where we had to hide, go away from our place where we lived, so nobody would know that we got married in a synagogue. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, you can see Steven Klein. He was born in 1953, 2nd of September. On this picture, he was about two and 1/2 years old. That was taken back in Hungary, visiting Steven's grandmother, who is my wife's mother. She was living in Mako with her husband. And the kid was their biggest enjoyment. And all summer, he spent down there, and he was playing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is your son.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our son, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is our family. On the top, you can-- top left, you can see our son. He grew up a little bit from the previous picture. His name is Steven Klein. There is, next to him, is standing his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Bernie-- Bernadine Klein. And that's me on the left bottom corner. Next to me, sitting, my wife Susan Klein. And there is, next to us, standing our granddaughter, who is now 15 years old. This picture was taken about three years ago, so she was about 12 years old. This picture was taken in Kenosha, Wisconsin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name and your granddaughter's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sandor Klein, and the granddaughter name is Kelli Klein, K-E-L-L-I.
